results.abstract: <jats:p>Artificial intelligence is profoundly influencing various facets of our lives, indicating its potential to significantly impact sustainability. Nevertheless, capturing the productivity gains stemming from artificial intelligence in macro-level data poses challenges, leading to the question of whether artificial intelligence is reminiscent of the “Solow paradox”. This study employs micro-level manufacturing data to investigate the impact of artificial intelligence on firms’ productivity.

results.abstract: <jats:p>We first calculate the global value chains (GVCs) participation indices from the Full Eora data of the Eora Global Supply Chain Database by employing advanced calculations methodologies. Then, we examine the productivity and profitability effects of various GVC participation measures by using the dataset on top 1000 Turkish industrial enterprises. results.title: The Role of AI in Transforming Labor Markets: Productivity Gains Versus Job Displacement
results.venue: Next Generation Journal for The Young Researchers
results.abstract: <jats:p>Artificial Intelligence (AI) is reshaping labor markets globally, acting as a dual-edged force of transformation. While it promises unprecedented productivity gains and economic growth, it simultaneously raises concerns about job displacement and workforce polarization. This research examines the role of AI in transforming labor markets, analyzing its impact across industries and demographic groups. By evaluating case studies and leveraging econometric modeling, the study highlights the

results.title: The aggregation paradox of AI
results.abstract: <jats:p>This research brief examines why strong productivity gains from artificial intelligence (AI) observed at the task and individual worker level have not yet translated into measurable productivity growth at the firm, sectoral or macroeconomic level. It reviews emerging evidence on AI adoption, diffusion and workplace transformation, while exploring the economic and institutional conditions that shape how productivity gains scale across the economy.</jats:p>

results.abstract: <jats:p>AI coding tools produce a well-documented effect at the individual level: they compress the skill distribution, raising the performance floor while leaving the ceiling largely unchanged. Brynjolfsson et al. (2025) found bottom-quartile workers improved 36% while top-quartile workers showed no significant change. Cui et al. results.title: From 1h a day to +1% productivity Why AI Saves You an Hour and Your Company Gains Nothing
results.abstract: <jats:p>Generative AI produces real and measurable gains at the level of individual tasks. Yet those gains do not automatically translate into firm-level or economy-wide productivity growth. This paper argues that the missing link is organizational plasticity, approximated through four structural axes: legibility, modularity, routing substitutability, and redesign authority. These axes are proxied using existing management surveys and combined into a composite framework that captures a firm's ca

results.abstract: <jats:p>The question is not whether AI will increase productivity. The evidence now shows that it will. The question is whether individual organizations and teams will capture those gains-or consume them in recovery, rework, and ungoverned autonomous action.</jats:p>

results.abstract: <jats:p>This study explores the use of ChatGPT for social media marketing strategies and its potential impact on employment. We take stock of the literature on productivity gain and the automation of work to unpack the role of AI in devising social media marketing strategies. To this end, we carried out in-depth, semi-structured interviews with 20 social media professionals who informed a follow-up experiment using ChatGPT to generate social media marketing strategies for two companies. results.title: The Economic Impact of Digital Twins in Architecture: Quantifying Productivity Gains, Reduced Rework, and Lifecycle Cost Savings
results.abstract: <jats:p>Digital twin (DT) technology is rapidly transforming the architecture, engineering, and construction (AEC) industry, promising significant economic benefits across project lifecycles. This paper systematically examines the economic impact of digital twins in architecture, with a focus on quantifying productivity gains, reductions in rework, and lifecycle cost savings.
